title: The Spinsterï¿½s Sweet-Arts
content:
1
Milk for my sweet-arts, Bess! fur it mun be the time about now
When dolly cooms in froï¿½ the far-end close wiï¿½ her paï¿½ils froï¿½ the cow.
Eh! tha be new to the plaï¿½ceï¿½thouï¿½rt gaï¿½pinï¿½ï¿½doesnï¿½t tha see
I calls ï¿½em arter the fellers es once was sweet upoï¿½ me?

II

Naï¿½y to be sewer it be past ï¿½er time. What maï¿½kes ï¿½er sa laï¿½te?
Goa to the laï¿½ne at the back, anï¿½ looï¿½k thruf Maddisonï¿½s gaï¿½te!

III

Sweet-arts! Molly belike may ï¿½a lighted to-night upoï¿½ one.
Sweet-arts! thanks to the Lord that I niver not listenï¿½d to noï¿½n!
So I sits iï¿½ my oï¿½n armchair wiï¿½ my oï¿½n kettle theere oï¿½ the hob,
Anï¿½ Tommy the fust, anï¿½ Tommy the second, anï¿½ Steevie anï¿½ Rob.

IV

Rob, coom cop ï¿½ere oï¿½ my knee. Thou sees that iï¿½ spite oï¿½ the men
I ï¿½a kepï¿½ thruf thick anï¿½ thin my two ï¿½oonderd a-year to mysen;
Yis! thaw tha callï¿½d me es pretty es ony lass iï¿½ the Shere;
Anï¿½ thou be es pretty a Tabby, but Robby I seed thruf ya theere.

V

Feyther ï¿½ud saï¿½y I wur ugly es sin, anï¿½ I beï¿½nt not vaï¿½in,
But I niver wur downright hugly, thaw soom ï¿½ud ï¿½a thowt ma plaï¿½in,
Anï¿½ I wasnï¿½t sa plaï¿½in iï¿½ pink ribbons, ye said I wur pretty iï¿½ pinks,
Anï¿½ I liked to ï¿½ear it I did, but I brunt sich a fool as ye thinks;
Ye was stroï¿½kin ma down wiï¿½ the ï¿½air, as I be a-stroï¿½kin oï¿½ you,
But whiniver I looï¿½ked iï¿½ the glass I wur sewer that it couldnï¿½t be true;
Niver wur pretty, not I, but ye knawï¿½d it wur pleasant to ï¿½ear,
Thaw it warnï¿½t not me es wur pretty, but my two ï¿½oonderd a-year.

VI

Dï¿½ya mind the murninï¿½ when we was a-walkinï¿½ togither, anï¿½ stood
By the claï¿½yï¿½d-oop pond, that the foalk be sa scared at, iï¿½ Gigglesby wood,
Wheer the poor wench drowndid hersen, black Sal, esï¿½ed been disgraï¿½ced?
Anï¿½ I feelï¿½d thy arm es I stood wur a-creeï¿½pin about my waï¿½ist;
Anï¿½ me es wur allus afearï¿½d of a manï¿½s gittinï¿½ over fond,
I sidled awaï¿½y anï¿½ awaï¿½y till I plumpt foot fust iï¿½ the pond;
And, Robby, I niver ï¿½a liked tha sa well, as I did that daï¿½y,
Fur tha joompt in thysen, anï¿½ tha hoickt my feet wiï¿½ a flop froï¿½ the claï¿½y.
Ay, stick oop thy back, anï¿½ set oop thy taï¿½il, tha may gie ma a kiss,
Fur I walkï¿½d wiï¿½ tha all the way hoam anï¿½ wur niver sa nigh saï¿½yinï¿½ Yis.
But wa boath was iï¿½ sich a clat we was shaï¿½med to cross Gigglesby Greeï¿½n,
Fur a cat may looï¿½k at a king thou knaws but the cat mun be clean.
Sa we boï¿½th on us kep out oï¿½ sight oï¿½ the winders oï¿½ Gigglesby Hinnï¿½
Naï¿½y, but the claws oï¿½ tha! quiet! they pricks clean thruf to the skinï¿½
Anï¿½ wa boï¿½th slinkt ï¿½oï¿½m by the brokken shed iï¿½ the laï¿½ne at the back,
Wheer the poodle runnï¿½d at tha once, anï¿½ thou runnï¿½d oop oï¿½ the thack;
Anï¿½ tha squeedgï¿½d my ï¿½and iï¿½ the shed, fur theere we was forced to ï¿½ide,
Fur I seed that Steevie wur coominï¿½, and one oï¿½ the Tommies beside.

VII

Theere now, what art ï¿½a mewin at, Steevie? for owt I can tellï¿½
Robby wur fust to be sewer, or I mowt ï¿½a liked tha as well.

VIII

But, Robby, I thowt oï¿½ tha all the while I wur chaï¿½nginï¿½ my gown,
Anï¿½ I thowt shall I chaï¿½nge my staï¿½te? but, O Lord, upoï¿½ coominï¿½ downï¿½
My bran-new carpet es fresh es a midder oï¿½ flowers iï¿½ Maï¿½yï¿½
Why ï¿½ednï¿½t tha wiped thy shoes? it wur clatted all ower wiï¿½ claï¿½y.
Anï¿½ I could ï¿½a cried ammost, fur I seed that it couldnï¿½t be,
Anï¿½ Robby I gied tha a raï¿½tin that sattled thy coortin oï¿½ me.
Anï¿½ Molly anï¿½ me was agreed, as we was a-cleaninï¿½ the floor,
That a man be a durty thing anï¿½ a trouble anï¿½ plague wiï¿½ indoor.
But I rued it arter a bit, fur I stuck to tha moor na the rest,
But I couldnï¿½t ï¿½a lived wiï¿½ a man anï¿½ I knaws it be all fur the best.

IX

Naï¿½yï¿½let ma stroï¿½k tha down till I maï¿½kes tha es smooth es silk,
But if I ï¿½ed married tha, Robby, thouï¿½d not ï¿½a been worth thy milk,
Thouï¿½d niver ï¿½a cotchï¿½d ony mice but ï¿½a left me the work to do,
And ï¿½a taï¿½en to the bottle beside, so es all that I ï¿½ears be true;
But I loovs tha to maï¿½ke thysen ï¿½appy, anï¿½ soa purr awaï¿½y, my dear,
Thou ï¿½ed wellnigh purrï¿½d ma awaï¿½y froï¿½ my oï¿½n two ï¿½oonderd a-year.

X

Sweï¿½rin agean, you Toms, as ye used to do twelve year sinï¿½!
Ye niver ï¿½eï¿½rd Steevie swear ï¿½cepï¿½ it wur at a dog coominï¿½ in,
Anï¿½ boath oï¿½ ye mun be fools to be hallus a-shawinï¿½ your claws,
Fur I niver cared nothink for neitherï¿½anï¿½ one oï¿½ ye deï¿½d ye knaws!
Coom give hoï¿½ver then, weant ye? I warrant ye soom fine daï¿½yï¿½
Theere, dig downï¿½I shall hew to gie one or tother awaï¿½y.
Canï¿½t ye taï¿½ke pattern by Steevie? ye shant hew a drop froï¿½ the paï¿½il.
Steevie be right good manners bang thruf to the tip oï¿½ the taï¿½il.

Xl.
Robby, git down wiï¿½tha, wilt tha? let Steevie coom oop oï¿½ my knee.
Steevie, my lad, thou ï¿½ed very nigh been the Steevie fur me!
Robby wur fust to be sewer, ï¿½e wur burn anï¿½ bred iï¿½ the ï¿½ouse,
But thou be es ï¿½ansom a tabby es iver patted a mouse.

XII

Anï¿½ I beï¿½nt not vaï¿½in, but I knaws I ï¿½ed led tha a quieter life
Nor her wiï¿½ the hepitaph yonder! ï¿½A faï¿½ithfnl anï¿½ loovinï¿½ wife!ï¿½
Anï¿½ ï¿½cos oï¿½ thy farm by the beck, anï¿½ thy windmill oop oï¿½ the croft,
Tha thowt tha would marry ma, did tha? but that wur a bit ower soft,
Thaw thou was es soï¿½ber es daï¿½y, wiï¿½ a niced red faï¿½ce, anï¿½ es cleï¿½n
Es a shillinï¿½ fresh froï¿½ the mint wiï¿½ a bran-new ï¿½eï¿½d oï¿½ the Queeï¿½n,
Anï¿½ thy farminï¿½ es cleï¿½n es thysenï¿½, fur, Steevie, tha kepï¿½ it sa neï¿½t
That I niver not spied sa much es a poppy along wiï¿½ the wheï¿½t,
Anï¿½ the wool of a thistle a-flyinï¿½ anï¿½ seeï¿½dinï¿½ tha haï¿½ted to see;
ï¿½Twur es bad es a battle-twig1 ï¿½ere iï¿½ my oï¿½n blue chaumber to me.
Ay, roob thy whiskers ageï¿½n ma, fur I could ï¿½a taï¿½en to tha well,
But fur thy bairns, poor Steevie, a bouncinï¿½ boy anï¿½ a gell.

XIII

Anï¿½ thou was es fond oï¿½ thy bairns es I be mysen oï¿½ my cats,
But I niver not wishï¿½d fur childer, I hevnï¿½t naw likinï¿½ fur brats;
Pretty anew when ya dresses ï¿½em oop, anï¿½ they goï¿½s fur a walk,
Or sits wiï¿½ their ï¿½ands afoor ï¿½em, anï¿½ doesnï¿½t not ï¿½inder the talk!
But their bottles oï¿½ pap, anï¿½ their mucky bibs, anï¿½ the clats anï¿½ the clouts,
Anï¿½ their mashinï¿½ their toys to pieï¿½ces anï¿½ maï¿½kinï¿½ ma deaf wiï¿½ their shouts,
Anï¿½ hallus a-joompinï¿½ about ma as if they was set upoï¿½ springs,
Anï¿½ a haxinï¿½ ma hawkard questions, anï¿½ saï¿½yinï¿½ ondecent things,
Altï¿½ a-callinï¿½ ma ï¿½huglyï¿½ mayhap to my faï¿½ce, or a teï¿½rinï¿½ my gownï¿½
Dear! dear! dear! I mun part them Tommiesï¿½Steevie git down.

XIV

Ye be wuss nor the men-tommies, you. I tellï¿½d ya, na moor oï¿½ that!
Tom, lig theere oï¿½ the cushion, anï¿½ tother Tom ï¿½ere oï¿½ the mat.

XV

Theere! I haï¿½ masterï¿½d them! Hed I married the Tommiesï¿½O Lord,
To loove anï¿½ obaï¿½y the Tommies! I couldnï¿½t ï¿½a stuck by my word.
To be horderï¿½d about, anï¿½ waï¿½ked, when Molly ï¿½d put out the light,
By a man coominï¿½ in wiï¿½ a hiccup at ony hour oï¿½ the night!
Anï¿½ the taï¿½ble staï¿½inï¿½d wiï¿½ ï¿½is aï¿½le, anï¿½ the mud oï¿½ ï¿½is boots oï¿½ the stairs,
Anï¿½ the stink oï¿½ ï¿½is pipe iï¿½ the ï¿½ouse, anï¿½ the mark oï¿½ ï¿½is ï¿½eï¿½d oï¿½ the chairs!
Anï¿½ noun oï¿½ my four sweet-arts ï¿½ud ï¿½a let me ï¿½a led my oï¿½n waï¿½y,
Sa I likes ï¿½em best wiï¿½ taï¿½ils when they ï¿½evnï¿½t a word to saï¿½y.

XVII

Anï¿½ I sits iï¿½ my oï¿½n little parlour, anï¿½ sarved by my oï¿½n little lass,
Wiï¿½ my oï¿½n little garden outside, anï¿½ my oï¿½n bed oï¿½ sparrow-grass,
Anï¿½ my oï¿½n door-poorch wi the woodbine anï¿½ jessmine a-dressinï¿½ it greeï¿½n,
Anï¿½ my oï¿½n fine Jackman iï¿½ purple a roï¿½binï¿½ the ï¿½ouse like a Queeï¿½n.

XVII

Anï¿½ the little gells bobs to ma hoffens es I be abroad iï¿½ the laï¿½nes,
When I goï¿½s fur to coomfut the poor es be down wiï¿½ their haï¿½ches anï¿½ their paï¿½ins:
Anï¿½ a haï¿½f-pot oï¿½ jam, or a mossel oï¿½ meï¿½t when it beï¿½nt too dear,
They maï¿½kes ma a graï¿½ter Laï¿½dy nor ï¿½er iï¿½ the mansion theer,
Hes ï¿½es hallus to hax of a man how much to spare or to spend;
Anï¿½ a spinster I be anï¿½ I will be, if soï¿½ pleï¿½se God, to the hend.

XVIII

Mew! mew!ï¿½Bess wiï¿½ the milk! what ha maï¿½de our Molly sa laï¿½te?
It should ï¿½a been ï¿½ere by seven, anï¿½ theereï¿½it be strikinï¿½ heightï¿½
ï¿½Cushie wur craï¿½zed furï¿½er caufï¿½ wellï¿½I ï¿½eï¿½rd ï¿½er a maï¿½kinï¿½ ï¿½er moï¿½n,
Anï¿½ I thowt to mysen ï¿½thank God that I hevnï¿½t naw cauf oï¿½ my oï¿½n.ï¿½
Theere!
Set it down!
Now Robby!
You Tommies shall waï¿½it to-night
Till Robby anï¿½ Steevie ï¿½es ï¿½ed their lapï¿½anï¿½ it sarves ye right.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
